rationale.R1.1: Gap statement (benchmark saturation on DAVIS/KIBA), contribution claim (RigorDTI curation + reference results), and thesis are all present but loosely integrated; tightening these is a standard expectation of draft improvement.
rationale.R1.2: Two live inconsistencies: (1) Sheridan et al. (2024) is simultaneously described as 'first introducing' the time-split protocol and as representing 'over a decade' of standard practice — these cannot both be true; (2) the inferential leap from a single RMSE-dispersion result to 'decisively resolves the long-standing question' and 'transformative advance' is unsupported by the evidence presented.
rationale.R1.3: Precise quantitative claims (0.02 RMSE clustering, 6× dispersion increase, ranges 0.71–0.73 vs. 0.84–1.31) are stated as findings but are unanchored to any table, figure, or appendix reference within the artifact; improving the draft includes surfacing whether these are properly grounded.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is the most literal reading of 'improve this draft' and is load-bearing; the text shifts uncontrolled between hedged scientific register ('we observe,' 'we find') and unhedged triumphalism ('transformative advance,' 'definitive new standard,' 'substantial leap forward'), creating tonal incoherence.
rationale.R1.7: The Sheridan et al. (2024) citation is the only named reference and carries a factual contradiction: a 2024 paper cannot be the origin of a practice described as 'standard for over a decade.' Priority attribution and reference accuracy require verification.

parsed.clarification_question: Should improvement be limited to writing quality and style (sentence clarity, tone consistency, flow), or should it also include substantive changes to the argument — for example, resolving the Sheridan 2024 citation contradiction, softening the overclaims ('transformative advance,' 'definitive new standard'), or restructuring the contribution framing?
